title: Sometimes the most expensive things don't mean the most profit margin
html:
TL;DR

  • November 2019 title-as-content note. Body empty. Whole thought: "Sometimes the most expensive things don't mean the most profit margin."
  • Earliest dated unit economics insight in the corpus — 16-year-old Yahir distinguishing price from margin before he had a word for either.
  • Likely surfaced from the finance class graph homework from late August, now metabolized into a usable rule.
  • Rhymes forward to the bromenu / rankett what-actually-prints-money questions and the ghost kitchen / ghost white label arbitrage thinking from the Austin era.
  • Companion to don't sell the farm — both are early operator-instinct aphorisms.

Notes

One sentence, no elaboration. The note's value is that it's the first time in the corpus Yahir cleanly separates revenue from margin — expensive ≠ profitable. That distinction is doing real work in the business thinking of an 18 year old: he's been through one marketing budget vs sales homework cycle and has now extracted the generalized insight, which is the move that separates I read this in a book from I see this in the world.

The note doesn't name a target product. But late-2019 Yahir is watching his dad's Gtanos business get wound down (posted on Craigslist) — probably the live case where expensive trucks did not mean margin. The aphorism is likely abstracted from watching that fail.

Forward, this is the rule that animates the Bromenu which-restaurants-actually-print analysis, the Rankett partner-program math, and the ghost kitchen / whitelabel strategy arbitrage curiosity. The 18-year-old got the rule first; the 24-year-old built businesses on top of it.

Privacy note

None.

Connections

  • 2019 08 16 simplify finance graph — class-homework ancestor
  • 2019 09 28 gtanos craigslist — live case-study sibling
  • 2019 03 14 mr phillips substitute — operator-instinct sibling
  • unit economics · business thinking
  • bromenu · rankett · ghost kitchen · ghost white label · whitelabel strategy
  • gtanos · grants pass teen era
